selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
Look, I've been working in the garage for forty years, and I know one thing for certain—you can't force an engine to run if it's not built right. Same goes for these vaccine conversations. They've become a minefield, and honestly, I get anxious just thinking about bringing it up with folks at work or online. I spent about an hour scrolling through Facebook last night, and I saw three different arguments about vaccines that made my blood pressure spike. People are just so dug in these days.

Here's what I've learned works for me: I don't engage with the extremes anymore. When someone starts ranting online about their conspiracy theories or attacking people who made different medical choices, I just scroll past. Life's too short, and I've got enough on my plate without getting worked up over arguments I can't win. At sixty-three, I've made my own healthcare decisions based on what made sense to me and my doctor, and that's between us.

When conversations come up naturally—like at the shop or church—I try to keep it simple and respectful. I might say something like, "I did what my doctor recommended, and I respect that others made different choices." That usually ends it without things getting heated. The thing is, people don't really want to hear facts anymore. They want validation for what they already believe, and I'm not going to be the one to give them a hard time about it.

I'm not the type to organize debates or try to convince people. That's not who I am. I focus on what I can control—staying healthy, listening to medical professionals I trust, and not letting social media drama affect my peace of mind. My faith teaches me to be kind, even when I disagree with someone, so I try to remember that when tensions rise. The truth is, these conversations will keep happening, but they don't have to happen with me if they're just going to cause stress and division. I've got my running, my knitting, and my work to keep me grounded. That's enough.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: This persona is a 63-year-old widowed female mechanic in New York with low openness and agreeableness, moderate neuroticism and extraversion. Her low openness suggests resistance to new perspectives and preference for traditional viewpoints. Low agreeableness indicates she may be direct and less concerned with others' feelings. Moderate neuroticism combined with anxiety suggests stress about conflict. Her moderate extraversion means she engages socially but isn't overly outgoing. Upper secondary education and working-class background inform her practical, straightforward communication style. Her daily social media use exposes her to vaccine discourse. The combination of these traits suggests someone who experiences anxiety about heated discussions, prefers to avoid conflict, has firm personal beliefs, and uses practical coping mechanisms. Her hobbies and faith provide grounding. She would likely advocate for personal choice, respect for boundaries, and avoiding contentious online debates rather than engaging in persuasion or activism.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, practical, somewhat weary but respectful, grounded in personal experience, anxious about conflict, straightforward without being aggressive, faith-influenced kindness, working-class authenticity
citations: American Psychological Association - Big Five Personality Traits
Pew Research Center - Social Media and Health Information
response_parsed.safety_notes: This perspective acknowledges vaccine discussions without promoting medical misinformation. It emphasizes personal healthcare decisions and respect for boundaries while not dismissing vaccines or promoting hesitancy. The tone reflects genuine anxiety about polarization rather than anti-vaccine sentiment.
